summary: This post compiles reader responses to Spolsky's earlier essay arguing against rewriting code from scratch. Seth Gordon offers tips for reading others' source code, and Spolsky advises that reading code requires deliberately slowing down because code is far denser than natural language. Lou Montulli, a founding Netscape engineer and creator of Lynx, provides a firsthand account of how Netscape's decision to rewrite everything contributed to the company's downfall. Montulli confirms that years of accumulated bug fixes and protocol handling in ugly-looking code represented irreplaceable institutional knowledge. The post reinforces Spolsky's core thesis that rewriting from scratch destroys hard-won lessons embedded in existing code.
key_points: Reading code requires deliberately slowing down because it is far denser than natural language and contains implicit dependencies that must be traced
Many programmers lack the patience to read existing code carefully, which leads them to rewrite from scratch — but reading is still faster than rewriting
Lou Montulli, a Netscape founder, resigned partly because of the decision to rewrite everything, validating Spolsky's argument with a firsthand account
Ugly code that handles edge cases (like 5,000 lines for 60 FTP server types) represents years of accumulated knowledge that cannot be easily replicated
Netscape's rewrite decision was driven by real problems (Java integration, rapid development) but the response of starting over was disproportionate
money_quotes: I think the best way to read somebody else's code is just to SLOW DOWN… it's like deciphering a code, not like reading.
Code is much more dense than English, and contains 'secrets' that need to be deciphered by looking elsewhere.
It takes some skill to learn how to read code slowly and carefully, and many programmers are not patient enough (so they wind up rewriting the code from scratch).
But you have to remember that it's still faster to read than to rewrite!
It had taken 3 years of tuning to get code that could read the 60 different types of FTP servers, those 5000 lines of code may have looked ugly, but at least they worked.
I was unable to supply enough visions of doom to divert the effort.
There was good reason for a large change, but rewriting everything was a bit overboard to say the least.
key_insight: Reading existing code demands patience and deliberate slowness, but it is always faster than rewriting — and ugly, battle-tested code embodies years of hard-won knowledge that a rewrite will inevitably lose.
